sentences.text: Kitty Fischer, F-I-S-C-H-E-R.
K-I-T-T-Y.
Katarina Haasova.
H-A-A-S-O-V-A K-A-T-A-R-I-N-A.
No.
No.
Kitty was Slovish adjective to Katarina after my grandmother.
On the 12th of July, 1927, in Olomouc, present Czech Republic.
No, Czech Republic is only in existence for five years.
It was Czechoslovakia-- Moravia.
68 in July.
Well, that I can, because I only was born there because that was the only place available for a cesarean for my mother.
And we went to Puchov-- P-U-C-H-O-V-- in Slovakia, where we all lived in my grandfather's household.
My mother has married a Moravian house.
And I was born, as you can see, between the wars-- nine years after the end of World War I, and six years before Hitler came to power.
Completely assimilated, absolutely non-Jewish, with big Christmas trees-- I was the only Jewish child on Yom Kippur to go to school.
I was so ashamed.
I did not know what Yom Kippur was.
They called it very posh [NON-ENGLISH], the Day of Atonement.
But my father said that's for children to play behind the synagogue.
And I had to go to school.
And everything went smooth, assimilated-- French governesses, German governesses.
No Jewish friends.
To my mother, every thing Jewish was poor.
I was invited to a Seder.
My mother would say that's for poor people.
I was invited for Purim.
That's for poor people.
My mother was Hungarian, and a very snobbish, uppity lady.
And I was brought up exactly the same way.
No jargon-- [YIDDISH] that was Yiddish.
If I said, [YIDDISH].
And suddenly came 1939, the Slovak Republic was brought into life on the 13th of March, 1939.
That I don't know, and I don't remember, and I don't want to know.
Probably by merchants there-- I was the fifth generation born there.
And my father had only one leg.
He was a highly decorated soldier in the First World War, and lost his leg at the Italian front.
And my happiest childhood memories, there were two-- the German governesses, who came all from the Sudetenland.
And they were telling us, Kitty, you have nothing to worry.
When our Fuhrer will come into power, I will protect you, because you speak such a beautiful German.
You will never pose as a Jew.
I was so convinced that they will come to my rescue that there is a great percentage of my survival stamina in Auschwitz.
I waited for Tant and Mimi to turn up suddenly and get me out of the mess.
So the second happiest memory was Sunday afternoon, was chamber music.
My father played the cello, which was taller than him.
My mother was singing.
Frau Nettle, German, played the piano.
And Mr.
Outie, a Slovak policeman, the flute.
And every Sunday afternoon, to my greatest boredom, we sat in little white nursery chairs with my sister, Eva, with little white aprons.
Everybody was swimming, or playing balls, or doing anything.
We had to listen to, and always the end of the concert, my father played from the [NON-ENGLISH] by-- on cello.
And that was-- and my mother sang it-- and that was the end of the performance.
Yes, he had his own chamber.
That was his chamber quintet, which he brought into life.
And he came by the House of Rothschild, from Vienna.
He was a foreign language correspondent.
And because he was a war-damaged person, he got preferential treatment, and was sent to Puchov, a break off of Slovakia.
And then he heads to manage a factory, ironically, for asbestos roof covering.
And he had a fiacre with his own couture, because he couldn't walk.
Fiacre is in Vienna, is a [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know how you call it.
It's a carriage, a fiacre-- that's very typical, big wheels.
With one horse.
His name was Bartol.
And with that, he went to the post office.
And he started WZO-- he was a great Zionist.
My father had no religion, but he was a Zionist.
He spoke Hebrew.
He was at the second Zionist conference in Basel.
He was born in 1888, and he came important to this Hungarian-speaking Jewish community, who did not know where God lives.
And he was a general manager, very high position, to this asbestos factory, started his chamber music, continued which he did in Vienna.
And was probably bored stiff.
And he met my mother, 16 years younger than him, and-- Helena.
Very spoiled, Budapest-educated.
Very rich.
She had all the money, which I found out during the years, and he had a war pension, and he entered the business of my grandfather as a bookkeeper.
My grandfather didn't like him.
I remember that because my grandfather was used to chalk up that people owed him on the counter or under the counter or give it to them on credit and not ask for their money back.
He was loved and cherished by the peasants like a saint.
Normal people, when I say middle-class people, didn't come near him because he was a foul-mouthed old Slovak Jew, who had bacon for breakfast.
But on Yom Kippur he was in slippers all day long in the synagogue, and came home in the evening.
And with my father, they uttered the only sentence of the year, [NON-ENGLISH].
And they had a slivovitz, and the proceedings then went on.
And so then one day, Mrs.
Nettle came in 1938-- when I was 11-- and said to my father, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I am very sorry.
I belong to the Sudeten Partei, and they don't like me coming into Jewish households.
I can't play your piano on Sunday any more.
So my father said, we carry on without the piano.
And my mother played it and sung at the same time.
Can I just stop you for a second?
When you say your father worked for your grandfather's business, what kind of business was that?
Import business of all-- it was called Southern Produce, like coconuts, and figs, and dates.
And the retail outlet were two big trucks, and they delivered to small corner shops all in the surroundings of Puchov, goods every week.
So they had an import-export outlet.
But they had a big pub after the war, and they also rented out these fiacres, as I call them.
For every wedding, they had to book with Mr.
Haas, my grandfather.
And we used to get cakes as a present.
Very central.
2,000 people.
All together, and 800 Jews.
And three came back.
All--all of them.
Not in a big way, no.
But little, rather poor, corner-shop Jews.
Mrs.
Buchelder, and Mrs.
Bischla, the kosher butcher.
I don't know what he was kosher for.
I did not realize that there was a shochet there.
I did not-- I did not know anything.
That was for poor people-- that was evil.
And in '39, when I was thrown out of school, suddenly all of this glamour was gone, because I was different.
Suddenly, then it struck me that I am not the Kitty Haas anymore at all.
And I decided on two things.
Number one, I'm going to write a book on all this nonsense, because somebody has to record it.
That was a commitment which I made.
And number two, that I'm going to become a rebel.
No way in the world am I going to play this dirty game of the grown-ups.
If this is the grown-up world, they can keep it.
No, there were about six or eight of us in different classes.
But I did not have to go to Jewish scripture-- my father didn't approve of it.
So they went for the alav bet, and they-- they're cliquey.
I was not liked.
I was foul-mouthed.
I was spoilt.
I was very intelligent.
I wrote into children's magazines in German and in Slovak.
I made poetry.
I was different.
As a kid, I was different.
Always fat.
Nobody really likes me very much, because I was a product of these two very people who married so late in life.
My father from 1888, and I was born in '27.
How long is that?
How long is that? 1888 to 1927?
Yeah, 49 or something.
So I always had an old father.
And my mother had a little limp.
With difficulties, because I talked all the time, because I thought I know better than the teacher-- which was not very hard in a Slovak peasant school.
And my parents enrolled me in a Swiss school to go to Switzerland when I am 11.
That would have been in '38.
Not till 1939.
They wouldn't dare.
Not that they were not, but they wouldn't dare.
I was Kitty Haas.
I mean-- what is it?
Suddenly, out of the blue, they started throwing frozen horse shit after me in the street.
Suddenly I had to wear a Star of David.
So I decided that I'm not going to play this dang game.
And there was the premiere.
If you remember, they had the anniversary, here, the 60th anniversary last year of Walt Disney's Even the movie house belongs to my parents-- but you were not allowed to go there.
Not dogs, gypsies, and Jews were allowed into the picture show.
So I kept the bright idea, I take the Star of David off with my mother's manicure scissors.
And without the Star of David I must see And then I operated that Star of David off-- which had to be very firmly sewn on.
I had a catastrophe happen in my life.
My little jacket faded and I had a great big black Star of David on my black bust, and I locked myself in the toilet to sew it on again, that my mother doesn't find me out.
And I told this story a couple of years ago, here in Renwick High School-- I talk to schools quite often, when they do their Holocaust studies-- and they-- I said-- I make it always very funny.
I never speak about sad things.
I make it for children interesting how a child survived the Holocaust.
And they said, 'Miss Kitty, did you ever manage to see A little boy from Vietnam asked me.
I said, no, truthfully I never saw.
He said, 'I want your name and address after class, because I have a beautiful Walt Disney video, and there is the whole and I lend it to you.'
Yes, that's--no, the first was that I was thrown out of school.
September 1. 1st of September, when school starts in Czechoslovakia.
It was a decree from the-- from the Slovak government, that all solicitors had to be struck off.
There were destitute solicitors shoveling snow, because they were not prepared for not being a solicitor from one day to the other.
You were struck off.
Doctors struck off.
Children out of school.
I mean, if you follow the Holocaust procedure, this was the expulsion of the economy.
And you start with education-- you give them away the education, take away their lawyers, take away their solicitors, and our people will become smart.
And then they explained me-- I did not understand.
We were standing there, 1st of September, assembly, waiting to go to school.
My handbag, my school bag-- comes Mr.
Pollack, I so remember, a Czech-- that was bad news in Slovakia in these days, as bad as Jewish-- with a limp.
Everybody limped in my life.
So he comes there, and says, would please the following students step out.
And he reads, Buhola, Brill, Haas, Weiss, Weiss-- they were twins-- so six of us stepped out.
And he said, in the name of the republic, you are expelled from state public education forever.
My world-- I thought, I don't hear proper.
I, Kitty Haas, expelled from education?
He should be honored that I come to his stupid school.
So came the janitor with a-- with a box of old newspaper-- and limped.
He was also one of these war-damaged people-- and he laughed.
He laughed.
So I went to him, skipped the assembly, walked towards him and said, 'Why are you laughing?' He said, 'At you, because now you are going to be stupid, and we are going to be smart.'
And as I looked at him, I was 11 years old, I swore to myself that no one ever is going to call me stupid, ever, ever again.
And this will all come into my book.
And we had to, all right, march out.
I came home and I cried so bitterly.
It's the first time and the only time I remember I cried.
I never cried.
I had no reason to cry.
I cried, and I said to my father, You should have sent me to Palestine.
I should have gone on child aliyah.
That was a very, very lucrative thing for the Zionist movement.
You paid in Swiss francs or pounds sterling the full education of your child.
A pittance.
It wasn't very much money for wealthy people like my parents.
And for that, the child aliyah== which I think still exists-- took upon themselves to educate you.
You're talking about me.
I don't remember.
I remember that shops, by '39 it was getting very tough.
Shops had to have an Aryan manager who kicked them out as soon as he could.
My parents had a very nice [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] help in the shop, Jankol Hantag.
He's still in an old age home in Puchov.
Still there.
I write to him every Christmas.
And this Jankol Hantag, I think, from what I was told, was an illegitimate son of one of my uncles, and they gave him to my family to make him a nice businessman.
He drove the truck, he counted eggs, he fixed the butter, he was behind the counter, he was Jankol.
So my father, who was very intelligent, made a voluntary Aryanization.
And they gave him-- gave him, virtually-- 40% of the business, and kept 60%.
Which allowed my father to still be in the business with my mother.
My grandfather died 1940, when he was 80 years of age, in his sleep, luckily.
Because that would have been a catastrophe to tell him that there is an Aryanization in his shop.
Massive.
They were coming-- the peasants-- from near and far.
It was January.
Very cold, snow.
My father was again in a carriage, because he can't walk on ice.
And only two uncles were left.
Everybody else was deported already.
And these were dangerous times.
The police came to disperse the masses.
I have never seen so many people.
He was so much loved.
And to me, this shabby Jewish cemetery on which I have never been-- what should I do in a Jewish cemetery?
And so they brought a rabbi from the next town, ' because ours was gone already.
There was not worse-- it wasn't a [INAUDIBLE].
And they let down the-- the coffin.
And the rabbi said something in Hebrew, and he had a padlock in his hand.
And he closed the padlock, and threw the lock behind his head and the key in the grave.
I so remember.
I heard it plunk before the stone came falling down, that he shut off everything good or bad from his life, and he was gone.
And then to me, I thought to myself, this Judaism has got nice things in it.
I am going to investigate a little bit of it.
So what did I do?
I join the communist Hashomer Hatzair.
My mother hit the roof that I congregate around these rabble-rousers.
And the first order of the movement was, disobey your parents' guidance.
So that, I like very much.
I had to be home always 6 o' clock, because these were dangerous times.
Jews are not allowed in the street.
German soldiers everywhere.
I spoke to soldiers.
I spoke to people when I felt like it.
My mother was in permanent fear.

When I think back of her, I owe her, whenever I meet her again, a great apology, because to have me running around, roaming the streets and bringing news and going from here to there, and he wrote from [NON-ENGLISH], and he has gone to Poland, and I knew every gossip-- and--
Oh, a challenge.
I was a challenge.
It was a challenge.
I am going to put all this into my book.
I started a diary in Slovak.
I wrote it in Slovak, with dates exactly of transportations, and which date to where they went.
I was very political.
I remember Stalingrad.
I remember Leningrad.
I went exactly from what-- when the radio receivers were put on hold.
You could only listen to Radio Bratislava, so that you can't listen to London.
So this Jankol Hantag had a room by himself in our house, and he had-- we gave him our radio.
And my father went every evening-- I heard him whistle-- and he went down there.
He was such a brave man.
At 8:30, duh-duh-dum.
That was London calling.
And we lived by when Churchill was fighting on the beaches.
All this we heard illegally.
We could have gone to Dachau for listening to the radio.
These were, to me, exciting times.
And in my imagination, this will make a fabulous book.
I did not think I was in danger.
I did not feel sorry for my parents, because my father was still in the business.
My mother had to become a hausfrau, and she did a very good job of it.
She was a very industrious lady, and very clever.
And you were not allowed maids.
You were not allowed to go to the market before 11:00 in the morning-- where everything was wilted and gone.
Jews could only go.
So she had a back veranda where everybody came to her at 8 o'clock in the morning.
She worked for herself and for all her friends.
She suddenly became very cooperative and very nice.
She suddenly-- it hit her that we have to help each other.
She was not Frau Haas anymore.

She took it a lot better than I did, because to her it was suddenly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] great feeling of togetherness against the goyim who did the dirty by her, for how good she was to them over the years.
Well, I had nothing to do with them.
I went to a cheder.
We had to have-- we were allowed one Jewish class.
Everybody was there from 6:00 to 13:00.
Can you imagine?
One red-headed teacher-- Klein was his name-- [NON-ENGLISH], in despair.
I talked non-stop.
I was so excited, there was a war outside, and here I have to sit in a stupid class when I am going to Switzerland anyhow.
So I had nothing to do with these people-- these Jewish children.
My first birthday party, I had to invite them.
I was so reluctant to do so, I so remember.
It wasn't quite what I wanted.
I would rather not have.
My mother insisted.
I admire her for that.
She suddenly felt, this was her place, and this is how-- but she was older than me.
She saw it differently.
So came the Weiss twins, Fred Pulitzer is here, called Parker.
And two of the five-- out of the five, three survived.
What did you do to celebrate?
My birthday?
We had charred chocolate and a cake, and I got presents.
You can imagine during the war what I would get-- a fountain pen, maybe, or whatever.
And we had one bar mitzvah, Fred Parker who is here Pulitzer.
And I so remember he was singing so nicely in that synagogue, and suddenly there was [NON-ENGLISH], which I never was allowed to go.
I envied.
The And my father made white flag with a blue Magen David.
I did not realize what it meant.
And everybody was having flags.
And I was marching with what today is the flag of the state of Israel.
And it is many, many years ago.
And the ladies upstairs were throwing lollies down.
My mother told me, I forbid you to touch one lolly.
You have a shop full of lollies.
I donated a crate of lollies.
If you pick up one lolly, I beat you up.
And then suddenly, everybody started to scramble for the lollies.
So I scrambled more than everybody else.
I couldn't resist the temptation.
All these lollies, and I am not allowed.
So I was forever in the rebellion.

That started, unfortunately, and there was no one to control it, because when I think that my parents realized that I have no future in this Jewish school-- the teacher was taken away, the other children they are all deported-- I was there on my own with one girl.
She died of cancer last year in Israel.
When did the deportations begin?

So my mother was worried about me, and in '42, when it was really very tough, they had connections, and it cost an awful lot of money-- they sent me to a Catholic convent of the Pauvre Soeur de Notre Dame.
The Poor Sisters of Notre Dame, a teaching order in Trencin, in a Slovak-- nice, bigger town than mine.
And--
Money.
But my grandfather, who then was dead already, had a very good friend who was the Monsignor Carlei-- a Hungarian, I think he was a great anti-Semite.
But he brought the offering from the plate every Monday to my grandfather to change into notes.
In the biggest Nazi times, when the Gestapo was in the streets, Carlei sent two minstral boys, and they had to bring the offering.
And he didn't trust anybody at all with this honor.
